speaker: interviewee
text: How do you think I'm doing?

speaker: interviewer
text: You just told me that this gestapo-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You just told me in German what this officer said. No shooting of the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's-- it's a waste to put a bullet in a Jew. That was-- that was the [SPEAKING GERMAN]. It's a waste to put a bullet for the Jew. You going to start hanging.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a place where they made for hanging. And one side three, and the other side for three people together. So who was-- couldn't go to work, they-- everybody came back from work. Everybody came work. And everybody has to be lined up in lines all around, and nobody could move out from the line. Everybody has to watch.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they start taking out those people. And they took them out. They were all undressed, naked. Their clothes was putting away in a shack. And later, they checked the clothes, whatever it say, or whatever they did it. So-- and they start hanging now, and everybody was hanging already. And [GERMAN] was sitting on a high stool, high up. And his girlfriend, he had a Polish girlfriend, she was sitting next to him and watching. And his [GERMAN], his secondhand, his [GERMAN], were-- had a stick and was going on with the stick around pushing the dead person. [SPEAKING GERMAN] Look how he dances nice. How nice he dances. It was a really terrible thing. And that's it. And took a minutes. And later on after it's all finished, took everybody to get the food. A really lousy food, what you get--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect you psychologically?

speaker: interviewee
text: It affect everybody terrible. That was no-- there was no way-- there was no way in the beginning to run away or whatever. Because my wife, already, she wrote me a letter. But she didn't write in Polish. She wrote me in Jewish. [SPEAKING HEBREW] [HEBREW] is a Hebrew word. [HEBREW] means run away. [SPEAKING HEBREW]. She always used to write me. [HEBREW]. Now, I was figuring, but there was no place. You can't just run. Cause some people run away. One guy, one of my friends, ran away. He was lucky. He ran away and he went. He came back. It was a ghetto. He was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the other ones, there was some-- a couple of them, they run away and they caught them. Once they caught him, I didn't see it. But some people saw it, and they say, here, you know what they did with him when they caught him? What?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they caught him first with the dogs. They caught him. Bite him all over. And then he hit him with that whip all over till there was complete death, the commandant, Grzymeck. So-- and then he said something. From now on, one runs away, I'm going to take out from the line 10 people and shoot them right on the spot. But if one runs away. That mean one has to watch the other. Because nobody should run away. But it happened. When it came after eight months in the hard labor camp. I was sick for a while. Because the food was no good. Yeah. And the same time about-- this was 1942, about June. June or July. I said-- a letter came back from my mother, from Krakow. That's why I know that my mother was liquidated. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. She moved away and nobody knows where. That was the word what it say on the letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I know they must have liquidated Krakow, my mother and sisters. And that's it. How did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this will affect really bad. Really bad. One way the suffering, and the other way my poor mother. She had good already from all the years in Krakow, you know. The life was came back to her. We were all together. And everything was complete difference. Was city life, but we were hungry for city life. It was villages and villages. This was city life. Was difference in Krakow. It was a beautiful Jewish city.

speaker: interviewee
text: So affect a lot, but that's it. You have to go on. And when I got real sick, I couldn't go to work. So the monk step from the road. Forgot his name. So he say, you know what? You can't work. You can't do nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed behind a lot of it, my work. But I couldn't bend down so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was wrong with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was wrong? The stomach. Stomach was terrible. Terrible. Very sick, nauseous, and all of that stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Diarrhea?

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, so he said, you have to stay out. I give you a paper to the commandant, and he's not going to touch you. You stay in the barracks for three days, and then you'll feel much better. You don't have to go and make-- before, we were going to work. Just going outside, going on the truck, we have to run a half hour. Exercise with sick people. Run and run and run before going on the trucks and coming there and start doing hard work. So was not easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I say, I'm really afraid. He say, don't worry. I'll write it to commandment a letter that he's not going to touch you. I need you. So as I figure, all right. And when it came. I stayed. I didn't go to work. So I was a special place where the sick people were laying on the floor. In the morning, 5 o'clock in the morning, the commandant comes in, Grzymeck. Comes in like a wild animal in pajamas, long pajamas. And with a whip, open up the door. And he start screaming like a wild person.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] Dirty [GERMAN]. You don't want to work. You're lazy. And so and so, [GERMAN], [GERMAN], [GERMAN]. And I had that paper. I took it out from the envelope and I want to give him that paper. He was beating over the hand, everybody. And he beat. And I say Herr Commandant, here's a [GERMAN] from the [GERMAN]. He didn't hear me at all. He was screaming like hell. Beating me all over. And that paper fell down, and this is it. And I was out. Out. Outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came out under the wires all around by the end. Everybody take off their clothes, sit a whole day till everybody comes from work. I see so many I didn't expect it. I was runaway even in places-- I ran away where they were shooting after me, and I never let myself catch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Always running. Zigzag. The bullets were flying, but never. And I said, this is my end. No end to it. That's it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One of my uncle's brother, he died before they hung him. And later when they came from work, everybody lined up and-- by the gate gestapo coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your uncle was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. my uncle was not with me. He was in [INAUDIBLE] that time in a ghetto. But his brother was in Jaworow. He lived in Jaworow. So he was also in the same camp. His name was Danhirsch. Gavriil Danhirsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in this event, he died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In this event, before the hanging he passed away. And I was sitting next to him, and he passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you clarify what happened with him, and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was very sick. And even more sick than I was. So-- and I see in a while, after a while sitting there-- and we were sitting like from the morning till the afternoon, till late the afternoon, till people come from work. And he was-- you know, his breathe was so bad, breathing and breathing and breathing. And I try to talk to him. And I-- I saw that he is half-dead already. And then maybe a-- an hour before the hanging, I see he stopped breathing. And this was the finish. This was the finish. I felt really bad, but I figure maybe he is happier. Better off than I. Because I'm going to be hanged, and the hanging takes about-- what, it takes about, I don't know, three or four minutes. Something like that. You know, it's not easy. We saw how people were struggling. And being hanged. It was not an easy-- an easy task. It was real. I felt that he is better off than I'm going to be better off. Now, when everything was set already, and he is supposed to start hanging already, a lot of people want to go in the toilets. A father was standing like a son was hanged, and a son was watching while the father was hanged. You know, you couldn't run away. You couldn't do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were they hanging?

speaker: interviewee
text: The hanging was six people. Three and-- three on one side, three on the other. So-- oh, yeah. And then at once here comes the telephone. He had a telephone connected from the gate up to the place over here, to this stand when he was sitting high up. And he got up on and he start talking to-- to the police, Ukrainian police. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Nobody is going to be hanged. Everybody out in the barrack-- in the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what was happened, we didn't know why and what. But later, we find out. Because it came an inspection, came from Lemberg, big gestapo came inspection. They want to check how many work is done, how much supposed to be done, you know. So they want to check over that. So-- and he let everybody go in the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then when we came to the barracks already, they-- they-- they will start coming in also. Looking over. Looking over in the-- they were feeding. They had a kettle with soup and a little piece of bread. And they would come over, checking everybody. Everybody was getting the food. And going into The barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we find out that they came to check the books, how much is done, how much-- how many people is there, whatever, you know. And-- and they let me go. That's it. This was finished. And later at night, I slept over the night. So I talked to one of my best friends, a close friend I had. And I talked to him about running away. He say, I'm not staying here. Tomorrow I'm going out to work, and this is the finish. They're not going to hang me today. They're going to hang me tomorrow or after tomorrow. And while you were waiting to be hanged, all that day, what were you thinking?

speaker: interviewee
text: You thinking. I didn't have time to think. I just trying to-- to nap and to sleep, and not even to think. What-- because I know that's what's going to happen. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you scared?

speaker: interviewee
text: --nothing else. Yes. Scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe how scared you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: How scared? Shaking. Shaking. And thinking--

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: --of-- of Ruthie and the baby and that little boy, my stepson, and my whole family. Well, they were yet in the villages. They were yet in the villages. I know my mother and sisters. But still my two brothers. One was in Jaworow in-- no, one was not-- he was not in Jaworow already. He went to Russia. Later on, my brother, the painter, he went to Russia because they say you are from Krakow. You're not allowed to stay here. You have to go away. Or we'll take you to the army or you go to Russia to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- he married in Jaworow before you left. And later they had a child. He was [POLISH]. [SPEAKING POLISH] I had a letter from him. And he was on the [POLISH]. So I was thinking about the family, sure. And this was that. And you were naked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Everybody, the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Affect-- affect very much. But this-- this is that they say the [YIDDISH]. The Yiddish [YIDDISH]. My [YIDDISH]. [YIDDISH]. This is my-- the future, whatever. It's no future. The [YIDDISH] is, that's it. Yeah. But the next day, and he-- he said, OK. I'll go with you. Because I felt to going alone and I'm sick, I need somebody with me. And he was also from another city, but I knew him very good through his brother-in-law. I knew him very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he decided quietly, nobody should even hear. And I was talking with, we're going to run away. So it came in the morning. When I came in. The [POLISH] say to me. Scher, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. What are you doing here? You're supposed to be in the barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, you know what's happened. I was standing already for hanging because Grzymeck, the commandant, he kicked my paper out. He beat me, and this and that. He say-- I showed him. I showed him the-- the signs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what your body looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very painful. Very painful. Bloody, bloody. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could-- could you describe it further?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I showed him. And he say, too bad. Too bad. You want to relax--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how bloody were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was blood all-- all the signs, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you cry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't cry. No. Crying. I was a Polish soldier. What are you talking about, crying? No, I'm just kidding. You cry. It wouldn't help you to cry. It was pain, but you don't cry. You don't cry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he say, you want to sit down? And I say, I'll sit down for a while. But can I get somebody? I like to go-- can I get somebody to help me? I'm not going to stay in the barracks no more. Give me somebody to help me with the work, you know. So this way he can carry more stuff, material, with me. And I'm going to do the job. So he say, who can do this kind of work? I say, oh, sure, Godfried. Godfried. He is very handy. I know him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- he was actually no so handy. But anyway. So he say, OK. He called up Godfried. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Come here. And go with Scher. And you going to help him doing this kind of work. Now, from here, I have to go back about a couple hundred meters. Go back where I left. I was behind the work. And they start working, and working on the roads to building the roads. So I came back there. Soon I saw-- I don't see nobody from far away. I said, come on. Let's go. It was just happen that you-- we were going through the village, through a village, the highway. Going through a village. I said, let's go. And I explained him everything what I want to do. I have to explain we going to run away between the houses. We going to hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later at night, we going to work. We going to work-- walk through Przemysl, and we going to walk back to Jaworow. Now he say, how you know how to-- how to go? So many miles and miles away.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I say, I'll figure out how to go in my mind. How to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you tell me how you escaped, during those eight months you were there, could you describe what you ate?

speaker: interviewee
text: What we ate. The food, it was nothing. It was soup and grass. We-- like you wouldn't have barley or you wouldn't have maybe a couple potatoes. That's it. This was the soup, and a little tiny piece of bread, an inch big. That's it. It was really horrible. Horrible, with the food. It was really bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what-- how is it to be hungry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, to be hungry. It's rough. But you get a little used to it, you know, from the food. It's-- you know, to be so many months in a hard labor camp with all of that, you know, you get used to your body. Your body gets shrinked. And that's it. That's all what you get. And we know that's all what we going to have. Nothing else. And your body has to adjust. Adjust to this kind of food. Real bad food. And little food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's no such things like you can get milk or you can get a drink. You can get a drink water. Sure. And you mentioned the dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Till it came to the dogs and we run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. You mentioned that sometimes they-- they were-- they put dogs on the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- no.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. They were not putting dogs. This was later in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you are running away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm running away. Soon we came in, and I-- we walked in, and right away to a house about a quarter of a mile, to a house. And I saw a big stack of hay. So I say here we're going to dig in. Took out hay, put it away, and later start pulling back to be there till the night. So-- and the lady was just milking the cows. It was early in the morning. And she heard. She heard that noise.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she came out, and she say, you can't-- you can't stay in here. First of all, they come with dogs looking for you. They'll come first here, in this area, to look for you. And the dogs will smell you out. You can't stay here. If they smell you out, they'll kill us, me and my family, same thing like you. So you have to go. I say, where can I go? He say, go in the woods. And the woods were on a high little mountain. In the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, we have no chance. And so we went. We went, and he hold me by my hand because I was really sick. And then when we came in the woods, I have to find woods where there's really heavy wood. You know, something not just here a tree and here a tree. Very heavy, and go in, in like a ditch someplace, till I found a ditch. Found a ditch, go in there. So we grabbed off trees. From the trees, branches we grabbed off, and we start pulling in, going in the ditch, deep. And then pulling in the branches, closing up real good. And then we were sitting there. And an hour later, we heard already screaming and shooting and dogs barking. They came after us in the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, we heard this coming closer and closer and closer. And they came close to us. We heard the dogs. The dogs were jumping over us, over the ditch where we were. Jumping over us, and they didn't smelled us out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were sitting quietly. And then they ran further and further. And we were sitting there till it get dark. And when it start getting dark, we came out. And I start walking from the woods. We were going towards Przemysl. He had a cousin-- my friend had a cousin. Przemysl was also a ghetto. And he was on that Przemysl ghetto. So I say, you go to Przemysl and over there in the ghetto, you can get a little more food, you know. But the meanwhile, I saw-- I saw a light in a house, a poor house.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was an old man with his wife. And I knocked on the door-- on the window. I knocked on the window. And he say, who is it. I say, open up. We are Jews. We ran away. So he say-- he opened up. He say, what do you want?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say, we want something to eat. We want bread and whatever you got. A drink or whatever. He say, run away fast I don't want to have nothing to do-- you know, because we wouldn't go where there were young people. We wouldn't go in there. But they were two old people, so we were not afraid. He gave us bread and he gave us a drink. And we went. And I also ask him the directions, how to get to Przemysl ghetto. Now, before the Przemysl ghetto, I still didn't know exactly so I stopped in another house. There were Polish people there. And the Polish people were not so bad, because this was Ukrainian. The Polish people got a little suffering too for us, because the Ukrainian didn't like the Polacks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came out, and we ask him where's the ghetto. They say go the other way, and there you can pick up the wires and sneak in to the ghetto. And that's what we did. We pick up the wires. He let me in, and then I let him in. We came to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he ask a couple of Jewish policemen, Jew-- there was Jewish-- in the ghetto was always Jewish security, Jewish police. If they need to take to work, they just say give me so many-- so many Jews to work in this place, work-- so they didn't come into the ghetto and grab people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they have-- there was a Judenrat, call it a Judenrat. A president, and you had police where you send out-- taking the Jews to work inside-- there were all difference kind of places where they needed. So we-- he found-- he found his cousin. And the cousin took us in. He was living with other people also. Crowded. Very overcrowded. Every ghetto was overcrowded. And we had some food there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed there. This was in the afternoon. We stay there one day till it got dark. The second night, we can go out from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wouldn't remember. It's all happened like in the month of-- beginning of December or October or November, the end of November, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year, 1940-- '42. 1942. December, 1942. So yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went out at night. And later, we went out from the ghetto. I have to pass by-- a little further down, I have to pass by a bridge in the road. There's no way for me to pass the bridge because it was a lake going. I have to go through that bridge. And through that bridge, they have lights on. Now, before that bridge, I saw where they were making from flour a mill, where they were making flour. I went on to that place. And there were bags from flour there, laying bags from flour.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took the bag from flour for me one, and for him one. Maybe not. Two and two bags. I made his face with flour, and I made my-- my face with flour because we were-- our clothes was the clothes from the hard labor camp with stripes. So make him all the clothes. Dirty up. And put the bags. Just to get out through that bridge. And later, we walked only at night. Only at night. And this helped us.

speaker: interviewee
text: For some people were go and walking, they didn't recognize-- people going from the mill from work, you know. That's it. So later on, walking for two days. Everything was fine. Walking only at night. But then the third day, when I was walking through the village, it was 12:00 midnight. I was never walked before. And 12:00-- we didn't realize three guys, three Ukrainian guys, jumped on us. And they-- and I was talking to him only Ukrainian, not Jewish at all, or Polish. Only Ukrainian. And they jumped on us and they grabbed us. Oh. How-- somehow they recognized us or they recognized maybe the clothes. And they grabbed us on the [GERMAN], to come on way to the commandos. To the German commandos. Taking us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, here, listen. Why you going to do that, take us to the commandos? I was raised on a village, and I lived in a village between Ukrainian people. And I told them a story about my mother, how she was helping, you know, people. And they somehow think it over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they say, eh. We let them go. And then they let me go. I had yet tobacco hid in the barracks where my wife send me a pound of tobacco. And this was more than money in the years in the war. Because there was no money-- no-- no cigarettes or whatever to get.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I say, here. I got a pound of tobacco. I'll give you nice boys. And they say, OK. You a Jew, but you are not smart enough. You should be smart not to go through a village. Whatever you go, you want to go back to here or whatever you want to go, go in the back of the village around, and where there's no village you can go where the highway where nobody is going.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave me the idea. And that's what I did. No more going through a village. Now, when we went and we coming closer the fourth day, what's happened the fourth day already, what's happened, we were going through a-- there was a lake and there was a field where a guy was there, and we didn't see. We couldn't spot him, by a tree. And there was where they were-- he was feeding his horse there. He was there with the horse. And I didn't see him. And he jumped on us. Grabbed me and him, one with one hand and one with the other hand. Grabbed us. You Jew. You-- you [UKRAINIAN]. Ukrainian, [UKRAINIAN]. And you go with me. So anyway, before that I told my friend if anything will happen, anybody where there is one person want to grab us-- when there's two or three, for sure you can do nothing. But you try. But there's when one person, you don't let yourself go. I'll tell you what to do, and I'll give you a sound like we used to do in the woods. We used to do a click. Something like that. So he should know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I gave him the one side under his ribs right here. And he-- and Godfried gave him from the other side, and he's already laying on the floor. I knocked his teeth out this way. Keep knocking in till he was-- he didn't move no more, and kicked him with his feet. Kicked him in his face, and all over he was finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we got up and we were going. We were going. Finished him up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you kill him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Definitely. I don't think he could get up after he was all bloody and all-- and all the hits he got. So anyway, we went-- we didn't have even to run. I know he wouldn't get up. So-- and we went another day till we came to Jaworow ghetto. I have to go also through Lemberg. I had to go through the city of Lemberg. A city what it's unbelievable. On the way, we changed our clothes. There was an old house, and nobody just was lived there. So we went into that house, and there was a lot of clothes. We threw down our clothes and we put on all-- there was big. One was big, one was small, you know. And we put on difference clothes. We put it on, and we go through-- through Lemberg. Through the city. We have to go through the city. I don't know-- to miss a city like this, you can't go all around. It's very hard to get it. But I know where Lemberg is, where Janov is, and always go through-- to Jaworow. So I went through the city. Here is gestapo and here is gestapo, and here is police and here is police. But we try to sneak out in between, you know. They were going the other way, or this way. We wait. In summer with a big sweat. Sweating like hell. And we passed through Lemberg. Once we pass through Lemberg, then it was already easy. And we came to the ghetto at night on the fifth day. We came to the ghetto at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Couldn't get in through the bottom. We get in through the top. We crawl up, and went. And there comes out the Jewish police from Jaworow. So one guy, he was there. He say, what are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell me, what you doing here? I say, I just ran away from the concentration camp. And now we'll take up, also change tapes.
